era_context: The final years of Augustus's reign (27 BCE – 14 CE) in the Roman Empire. Augustus was in his mid-70s. In China, Wang Mang's Xin dynasty was in its early years (9–23 CE). In India, the Satavahana Empire was dominant. This was a period of relative stability in the Mediterranean world, though the succession question loomed large.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Asia beyond China and India

events.title: Augustus's reign in final years
events.description: Augustus, the founder of the Roman Empire and its first emperor, was in the final years of his reign in 2 CE. He had ruled for over 40 years and was in his mid-70s. The empire was at peace, with stable frontiers and a functioning administrative system. Tiberius was being groomed as his successor. Augustus would die in 14 CE, and the succession would pass smoothly to Tiberius, establishing the precedent for dynastic succession in the Roman Empire.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's reign dates (27 BCE – 14 CE) are well-established. The fact that he was alive and reigning in 2 CE is certain.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2 CE is extremely sparse. Almost nothing specific is known about this particular year. The only certainty is that Augustus was still alive and reigning. All other information about this year would be inferred from broader narratives of Augustus's reign and the early years of Wang Mang's dynasty in China. The vast majority of the world is absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Augustus's reign ended with his death in 14 CE
